html:
Pregnant woman stoned after weaning — Muhammad praises her repentance Women Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud #4442
"A woman from Ghamid came... 'I have committed immorality.' He said: 'Go back until you have given birth.' She came back... 'Go back and breastfeed him until you wean him.'... He ordered that a pit be dug for her, and he ordered that she be stoned. Khalid was among those who stoned her, and a drop of her blood landed on his face so he reviled her, but the Prophet said: 'Take it easy, O Khalid! By the One in Whose Hand is my soul, she has repented in such a way that if her sins were divided among the people, it would be enough for them.'"

What the hadith says

A woman confessed adultery. Muhammad sent her away twice — once for the birth, once for weaning — then had a pit dug and had her stoned. When an executioner recoiled at her blood, Muhammad rebuked him and praised her repentance.

Why this is a problem

The repeated delays make the execution deliberately premeditated. Muhammad could have declined to act on the confession or accepted her repentance as sufficient. Instead he managed a multi-year timeline until the logistics permitted — designing the procedure, not merely permitting it. The pit itself is a restraint mechanism engineered to prevent escape and maximize pain.

When Khalid's natural recoil at being splattered with blood earned a prophetic rebuke, the tradition normalized the act by correcting the executioner's squeamishness. The text frames this correction as a spiritual teaching moment.

Muhammad's declaration that her repentance was great enough for all of Medina does not substitute for her life — it justifies the execution. A God who accepts repentance does not require a public death to confirm it. Celebrating her spiritual state while proceeding with the killing reveals that repentance and execution operated as complementary, not alternative, outcomes.

Why it fails

Procedural delay before execution does not change the moral status of the execution — it makes it more premeditated. The "voluntary confession" framing does not neutralize a legal system that offered death as the outlet for religious guilt, one in which confession and execution functioned as a spiritual transaction. A legal tradition whose defining repentance narrative ends in a pit-stoning has disclosed something fundamental about its moral imagination: that divine acceptance requires a body in the ground.

Temporary marriage permitted by the Prophet, then banned Contradiction Abrogation Women Strong Abu Dawud #2072
"The Messenger of Allah forbade Mut'ah with women." [#2073]
"...we would engage in Mut'ah in the time of the Messenger of Allah..." [Bukhari parallel]

What the hadith says

Mut'ah — time-limited marriage in exchange for specified payment — was practiced by companions during several campaigns and then banned. Sunni Islam treats it as permanently forbidden; Shia Islam preserves it as valid. The contradiction is preserved in the hadith record itself.

Why this is a problem

A ruling on a sexual-access transaction changed. If Islamic ethics reflect timeless divine commands, they cannot flip on the permissibility of paying a woman for a fixed period of sexual access. Mut'ah is structurally indistinguishable from prostitution: a man pays a woman for time-limited access, and the "marriage" label does not alter the transaction's substance.

The Sunni-Shia split on this issue has persisted for 1,400 years with both sides citing the Prophet's own words. This is not a sectarian rounding error but a permanent canonical contradiction about the Prophet's actual ethics. The timing of the ban also tracks military convenience — Mut'ah was permitted when fighters were on campaign and forbidden when the community stabilized, suggesting the rule followed the military calendar rather than a moral principle.

Why it fails

The sequence some apologists describe — permitted, prohibited, permitted again, prohibited again — is preserved in the sahih canon itself. "Progressive revelation" does not resolve the fact that a sex-law changed multiple times. The Sunni-Shia split has endured precisely because the canonical record supports both readings. A divine sex-law whose final position cannot be determined from the tradition's own evidence is indistinguishable from ordinary legal development under conflicting testimony — and structurally, payment for time-limited sexual access has no coherent distinction from prostitution regardless of the label applied.

Breastfeed a grown man five times to make him a "son" Women Prophetic Character Strange / Obscure Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #2061
"He replied: 'Breast-feed him.' So she breast-fed him five breast-feedings, and he became like a foster-son to her. And so 'Aishah would follow that decision, and would command her sister's daughters and brother's daughters to breast-feed five times those whom 'Aishah wished to visit her, even if he was an adult..."

What the hadith says

When Quranic revelation ended adoption (Q 33:5), the adult Salim became legally a stranger to his foster-family. Muhammad's solution was for his foster-mother Sahlah to breastfeed him five times, creating legal kinship permitting him to remain in the household. Aisha adopted this as a general rule for any adult man whose domestic access she wanted.

Why this is a problem

The ruling is a physical absurdity treated as jurisprudence. An adult man does not nurse as an infant does; the act functions as a pure legal fiction, a ceremonial transaction divorced from any biological reality. Muhammad's own wives explicitly rejected extending the ruling, with Umm Salamah and others holding it was a specific dispensation for Salim alone. The hadith preserves this internal disagreement — within Muhammad's own household — about whether the ruling was universal.

Aisha nonetheless made it a general tool for circumventing sex-segregation law: Islamic sex-segregation is abolished for a specific man by a ritual of adult breastfeeding. The rule is simultaneously extreme and gameable by anyone with access to nursing relatives. The al-Azhar fatwa reviving this ruling in 2007, swiftly retracted under public outcry, shows the hadith is alive enough to be cited and embarrassing enough to be unusable — meaning it remains unresolved in the tradition's own terms.

Why it fails

The "specific dispensation" framing does not insulate the ruling from its implications: the tradition concedes that legal kinship can be established by adult breastfeeding, and classical jurists debated its conditions in explicit operational detail. The 2007 Egyptian fatwa demonstrates it remains live. The "symbolic not literal" reading is retrofitted — classical sources specify the number and nature of feedings in concrete terms. A legal category whose foundational case is "Muhammad permitted a grown man to be breastfed to solve a household access problem" cannot be defended by claiming rarity; the rarity is the apology, not the text.

Angels curse a wife all night for refusing her husband's bed Women Prophetic Character Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #2141
"If a man calls his wife to bed and she refuses, and he spends the night angry at her, the angels curse her until the next morning."

What the hadith says

When a husband wants sex and his wife refuses — for any reason — the angels of God curse her throughout the night until dawn. The curse is conditional on the husband spending the night angry.

Why this is a problem

The hadith eliminates marital consent as a recognized category. Tiredness, illness, grief — none qualify as grounds for refusal. The only morally permitted response is yes. The punishment is celestial and not human: God's own messengers are invoked as enforcers of a husband's sexual access, weaponizing metaphysics against women in a way no human law could replicate.

The trigger is the husband's emotional state. If he shrugs, no curse. If he sulks, she is cursed. A wife's standing before heaven is made dependent on her husband's mood and his choice to remain angry. This is not a balanced marital ethic but a one-way enforcement mechanism: celestial force applied to the wife's body, advisory hadith applied to the husband's consideration.

The hadith directly contradicts modern Islamic apologists who argue marital rape is forbidden in Islam. Both claims cannot be simultaneously true: a framework that attaches divine punishment to a wife's refusal cannot also meaningfully forbid coerced compliance.

Why it fails

The "balanced marital ethic" framing requires reading the hadith against its plain grammar. The text imposes a supernatural penalty on the wife for refusal; there is no equivalent angelic curse on a husband who is inconsiderate. Asymmetry is the finding: one party's preference is enforced by angelic curse, the other party's needs are addressed by advisory recommendation only. A system in which divine agents enforce the husband's access but not the wife's bodily autonomy is not balanced — it is one-directional enforcement wearing the costume of mutual obligation.

"Don't beat your wife like you beat your slave girl" — the analogy Women Prophetic Character Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #142
"...And do not hit your wife like one of you beats his slave girls."

What the hadith says

Husbands are instructed not to beat their wives the way they beat their slave girls. The comparison presupposes that beating slave girls is the unquestioned baseline — a routine practice the hadith takes for granted while seeking to spare the wife from it.

Why this is a problem

The reform is that wives should not receive slave-grade beatings. The slave girl still receives the full beating. The hadith introduces a differential cruelty rule: one category of woman is partially protected; the other remains the reference object, unchanged and unquestioned. Beating enslaved women is not critiqued — it is the analogy that makes the wife's relative protection comprehensible to the audience.

The rhetorical comparison only functions if every man in the audience could readily picture what "beating his slave girls" looked like. The hadith documents, without comment, that this was the ordinary experience of enslaved women in Muhammad's community. Some modern English translations soften "slave girl" to "servant" or "maid" — tracking contemporary embarrassment rather than the original Arabic, which is unambiguous.

Why it fails

The "graduated reform" framing concedes that these ethics are cultural-historical rather than eternal. The hadith's structure is a differential cruelty rule: the wife receives a concession; the slave girl is the unchanged reference point. The text does not say "do not beat anyone" — it says do not beat your wife like you beat the slave, which leaves the baseline beating intact. A reform that partially protects one class by reinforcing the reference status of another is not abolition; it is the restructuring of cruelty. Fourteen centuries of Islamic jurisprudence did not read this hadith as implicitly prohibiting the beating of enslaved women.

Abu Dawud's dedicated chapter: "Regarding Intercourse With Captives" Women Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud Book 12, Ch. 43
[Chapter heading] "Regarding Intercourse With Captives" [Abu Dawud Book 12, Chapter 43/44, containing rulings derived from Q 4:24 "...except those your right hand possesses"]

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud dedicates a named legal chapter to the rules governing sexual intercourse with female captives, treating the topic at the same register as rules for ablution or fasting. The chapter implements Quranic verses explicitly permitting sex with "those your right hand possesses."

Why this is a problem

The category exists. Whatever the individual hadiths say, the fact that Islamic fiqh required a dedicated chapter on this subject is the finding. Captive women were a standing sexual category in Muslim military life — not an aberration, but a matter requiring systematic legal guidance. The Quran authorizes the practice at Q 4:24, 23:5–6, and 70:29–30, so the hadith chapter is the implementation manual for verses the tradition cannot disown. Crucially, Q 4:24 explicitly overrides the normal prohibition on married women in the case of captives — the rulings govern sex with women whose husbands were alive and had simply lost the battle.

The chapter has been cited in the 21st century. ISIS invoked exactly these hadiths and Quranic verses to justify its Yazidi slave-rape program in 2014. Any defense of the hadith corpus must account for this application, which was not a misreading but a straightforward deployment of what the chapter says.

Why it fails

Regulation is not protection when the regulated act is non-consensual sex with enslaved women. The "compared to other ancient cultures" defense concedes the moral point: the practice was wrong. A chapter on how to have sex with captives ratifies the category of captive-rape regardless of the procedural guards surrounding it. An ethics that requires rules for intercourse with captives has already conceded the practice itself.

Allah's Throne rests on eight angelic mountain goats above seven heavens Science Claims Strange / Obscure Strong Abu Dawud #4723
"Then above that there are eight mountain goats. The distance between their hooves and their knees is like the distance between one heaven and the next. Then on their backs is the Throne... Then Allah is above that..."

"Allah is above His Throne, and His Throne is above His heavens... and it creaks on account of Him, as the saddle creaks on account of its rider."

What the hadith says

The universe consists of seven stacked heavens. Above them stand eight enormous angelic mountain goats. On the goats' backs sits Allah's Throne. On the Throne is Allah himself. The Throne creaks audibly under his weight, like a saddle under a rider.

Why this is a problem

This is a physical cosmology that modern astronomy has entirely retired. There are no seven stacked heavens, no supporting angelic goats, no creaking throne. Each element is a Bronze Age cosmological picture preserved intact in sahih-grade hadith. The creaking Throne implies weight and spatial physics: "creaks like a saddle under its rider" requires Allah to have mass and to rest on a load-bearing structure — directly in tension with Q 42:11: "there is nothing like unto Him."

Every apologetic exit costs something. Taken literally, the hadith is plainly false cosmology. Read metaphorically, sahih-grade hadith speaks in fantasy imagery about the structure of the universe, giving no anchor for which other cosmological descriptions are literal. Rejected as inauthentic, the collection's authority is undermined. The dispute between Hanbali scholars who affirmed Allah's literal spatial aboveness and Ash'arites who denied it traces directly to texts like this one — a theological schism of centuries produced by ambiguity the revelation itself could have resolved.

Why it fails

The metaphorical reading is a post-hoc rescue, not the canonical hermeneutic. The hadith was preserved in sahih collections precisely because it was taken to describe actual cosmological reality. If the imagery is metaphorical, the tradition has no anchor for determining which other hadith descriptions of Allah and the cosmos are literal — the metaphor-rescue, applied consistently, dissolves the corpus's cosmological content entirely. A revelation that required a thousand years of unresolved theological dispute to determine whether God sits on a goat-supported throne is not a revelation that spoke clearly on its most fundamental subject.

The stoning verse admitted missing from the Quran Contradiction Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #4418
"We used to recite: 'If an old man and an old woman commit adultery, stone them to death...' But the people said: 'We do not find the Verse of stoning in the Book of Allah.'"

What the hadith says

Umar and other companions testify that a verse prescribing stoning for adultery was originally part of the Quranic revelation. The verse is not in the present Quran. Umar explicitly worries that future generations will abandon stoning because they cannot find it in the text.

Why this is a problem

The hadith contradicts the Quran's own preservation doctrine. Q 15:9 declares: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." A verse the earliest companions remembered reciting is no longer in the text. Either the preservation promise failed, or the companions' memory was wrong — and the tradition preserves them asserting it was not wrong. The current Quran at Q 24:2 prescribes 100 lashes for adultery, not stoning. Classical law practices stoning anyway, citing hadiths about a verse that is no longer present — a capital punishment resting on the authority of a missing text.

Umar's anxiety is the tell: he feared future Muslims would not find the verse and would abandon the punishment. They did not abandon it — which means the practice survived the verse's erasure through hadith authority alone. This is a strange path for divinely ordained law: a Quranic mandate disappears, yet the lethal penalty continues on secondary evidence. The hadith — graded reliably and preserved in multiple collections — places two foundational claims in direct conflict: either the Quran is perfectly preserved, or this verse fell out.

Why it fails

"Abrogated in wording, retained in ruling" produces an uncomfortable result: a capital punishment operative in Islamic law whose Quranic basis was deliberately removed. The Quran's preservation guarantee is normally deployed to claim the text is complete and perfectly transmitted; the abrogation doctrine selectively abandons that completeness for this one verse. More fundamentally, Q 24:2 prescribes lashes for adultery with no mention of stoning. Two contradictory hudud penalties for the same offense — one present in the text, one traceable only to a hadith about a lost verse — cannot both be divinely ordained without admitting the legal system was being constructed under conflicting testimony.

Safiyyah's "dowry" was her own emancipation from Muhammad's captivity Women Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Abu Dawud #2054
"The Prophet freed Safiyyah, and made that (emancipation) her dowry."

What the hadith says

Safiyyah was a Jewish noblewoman captured at Khaybar after her father and husband were killed in the campaign. Muhammad freed her and married her on the same day — using her emancipation from the slavery he had imposed as the mahr (dowry).

Why this is a problem

Mahr is supposed to be the groom's gift to the bride. Here, Muhammad "gave" Safiyyah her own freedom — a right he controlled by virtue of having captured her. Ending an injustice you are imposing is not a wedding present. The act is legally structured as generosity, but the structure only makes sense within a system that first rendered her a slave whose freedom was his to grant.

Consent is structurally impossible in this scenario. Safiyyah had just witnessed her male relatives killed. She was offered freedom contingent on marrying Muhammad on the day of her capture. To refuse was to remain a slave. This is not a proposal — it is a coercive transaction with a matrimonial label. The Safiyyah case was regularized as legal precedent: Abu Dawud preserves it in the Book of Marriage, enabling future Muslim masters to free their slave-women and designate the emancipation as the mahr.

Why it fails

Elevating one woman from war-booty to wife presupposes the war-booty framework remains fully operational for everyone else — Safiyyah's special honor only makes sense against the backdrop of the ordinary slavery the other Khaybar women experienced. Her reportedly devout post-marriage life addresses outcomes, not the consent question at the moment of marriage. The "freedom as mahr" device is legally clever and morally incoherent: the same man who imposed captivity removes it as a gift. Under any framework that takes consent seriously, a proposal whose alternative is continued enslavement is coercion.

Abu Dawud's chapter: "How Were the Jews Expelled from Al-Madinah?" Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Abu Dawud Book 14, Ch. 22
[Chapter title:] "How Were the Jews Expelled from Al-Madinah?"

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud devotes a dedicated chapter to documenting the expulsion of the Jewish tribes of Medina and ultimately of the entire Arabian peninsula, cataloging Muhammad's role and the procedures used to carry out the removals.

Why this is a problem

The chapter heading concedes the fact. The Jews were expelled; the collection asks only how, not whether the expulsion was just. The organizing question is procedural, not moral — and that framing has driven fourteen centuries of application. Saudi Arabia's modern policy forbidding non-Muslim worship in the Hijaz traces partly to this expulsion as precedent. The chapter also sits in tension with the Quranic treatment of People of the Book: Q 5:5 permits Muslim men to marry Jewish and Christian women, while the physical expulsion of those same communities from the peninsula was never harmonized with that inclusion.

The precedent has been reactivated repeatedly — from Umayyad-era expulsions through the 20th-century displacement of Jews from Arab countries — with the "Jews out of Arabia" pattern drawing on this textual anchor. A tradition that catalogs removal procedures without questioning removal has already treated removal as the conclusion.

Why it fails

The "specific breaches" argument works case-by-case but collapses cumulatively: three Jewish tribal groups were expelled or massacred within a few years, leaving Medina entirely cleared of its Jewish population. The chapter heading's neutrality is the tell — the tradition's organizing question about a community's removal is procedural, not ethical, meaning removal was already treated as the settled conclusion. Contemporary Saudi state policy and fourteen centuries of Islamic jurisprudence have applied the exclusion principle rather than the case-by-case exception framing.

"Do not force your slave girls into prostitution" — and the implied baseline Women Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud #3454
"...do not force your slave girls..."

What the hadith says

The ruling — echoing Q 24:33 — prohibits masters from forcing their enslaved women into prostitution for financial gain. The master's own sexual access to the same women is untouched by the prohibition.

Why this is a problem

The reform presupposes the practice. A prophetic prohibition on forcing slave women into prostitution exists because masters were doing it — frequently enough to require a ruling. The prohibition targets pimping, not possession: a master may not send his slave woman to be used by others for money, but Q 4:24 explicitly permits his personal sexual use of her. The boundary drawn is commercial, not ethical.

Q 24:33 adds a conditional that is structurally damning: "do not force them into prostitution if they want to preserve their chastity." Divine protection of an enslaved woman's body is made conditional on her own stated preference — a preference that cannot be meaningfully expressed under conditions of total power asymmetry. The Quran ties her protection to a free choice she cannot make.

Why it fails

A moral advance that says "do not force your slave women into prostitution" while leaving the master's personal sexual access intact is not abolition — it is a protocol for slavers. The "if they want chastity" conditional is the structural problem: it makes divine protection of an enslaved woman's body depend on her expressed preference in a context where no preference is free. The reform cleans up the worst commercial edge of the practice while leaving the practice — including unrestricted personal sexual access — entirely intact. No classical jurist read these texts as implying eventual prohibition of concubinage; the trajectory-to-abolition framing is retrospective apologetics.

Uraniyyin: hands cut, eyes branded with heated nails, denied water to die Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Abu Dawud #4364
"He ordered that their hands and feet be cut off and their eyes be branded, then they were thrown in the Harrah where they asked for water but were not given any." (Sahih)

"He ordered that nails be heated, then he blinded them and cut off their hands and feet, and he did not cauterize them." (Sahih)

What the hadith says

A tribal group came to Medina, converted, recovered from illness using camel urine, then apostatized, murdered the herdsman, and stole camels. Muhammad's sentence: amputate hands and feet, blind them with heated iron nails — deliberately without cauterization — then abandon them in the volcanic desert to die of thirst.

Why this is a problem

The torture exceeds even the prescribed Islamic penalty. Classical law prescribes cross-amputation or execution for highway robbery — not both, plus blinding, plus dehydration. Muhammad's sentence deliberately surpassed the Quranic warrant offered in its defense: Q 5:33 prescribes cross-amputation, exile, or crucifixion, not heated-nail blinding or death by thirst. The second narration specifies that nails were heated but cauterization was withheld — the detail that normally seals wounds. Maximizing suffering was the stated design, not a side effect.

Water was withheld as part of the punishment, not incidentally. The text records they "asked for water but were not given any" while lying in the volcanic desert. The thirst was the killing mechanism, deliberately maintained. This creates an internal contradiction with Muhammad's own hadith prohibiting mutilation and torture — a contradiction the tradition has never cleanly resolved, leaving two bodies of Prophetic precedent pointing in opposite directions.

Why it fails

The mirror-punishment defense fails because the hadith records deliberate maximization of suffering — nails heated, cauterization withheld, water denied. That is not proportionate retaliation; it is systematic cruelty by design. The "superseded by later hadith" argument requires choosing which Prophetic hadith governs, and fourteen centuries of scholarship have not reached consensus. The Abu Dawud version remains in the canonical record as sahih. Whatever the apologist's preferred reading, the text itself records Muhammad ordering torture, prolonged suffering, and slow death by thirst.
